titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 16 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to his wife, Lillian (1880-1968) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/63

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Nov. 16 Only a buff slip today for
you in spite of your many
most welcome letters which
make me hungrier than ever.
I am simply longing to hear
your decision but feel it is
quite useless for me to try &
help you in it. Letters are
no use in a case like that. It is very nice of Genl Powell
to write as he does but there is
absolutely no chance of his
prophecies coming true in my
case. Please make up your mind
to that as it make the future
easier. In certain circumstances
one might have had a chance,
but a concatenation of accounts
including Gen' N makes it quite
impossible & what is worse it will
be impossible to explain why
later. The man with a
grievance is a bore so wemust
keep off it. I really am not surprised
at Don's affection & am very
sorry I am not to hear that
he is doing his best to take
a b w's place even it it only
amounted to foot drill, & a little
arm exercise. October's rent was paid
beforehand by a b w ! He knows
that a p.r. doesn't really need
more than a burrow & can
always borrow one from many
friends but I like to think
she is in one provided by a b w. M Carson has gone to one
of the new Reserve battns — a
distinct asset for us.
